macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 40: Slaughtering for Idolatry (Avodah Zarah) and the Status of the Slaughterer

micro_segments.title: Overview of the daf: Distinction between past issues (non-Jewish slaughterer) and current focus (Jewish slaughterer with idolatrous intent)
The Mishnah: Slaughtering for mountains, hills, oceans, rivers, and wildernesses

macro_segments.title: Two Slaughterers with Conflicting Intentions: Can One Participant's Intent Invalidate the Act?

micro_segments.title: Mishnah case: Two people holding the knife, one with idolatrous intent and one with proper intent; the slaughter is invalid
Parallel to earlier rulings: Can a partial participant's thoughts invalidate, similar to partial ownership?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Analysis: Distinguishing Offerings to Idols from 'Offerings to the Dead' (Zivchei Meitim)

micro_segments.title: Gemara response: Why does the Mishnah use the weak term 'psulah' rather than stating it is forbidden in benefit (assur b'hana'ah)?
Baraita: Slaughtering for mountains, hills, sun, moon, stars, Michael the angel, and even tiny worms are all considered zivchei meitim (offerings to the dead), forbidden in benefit
Abaye's explanation: The Mishnah addresses offering to the mountain itself, while the Baraita addresses offering to the spirit or angel of the mountain

macro_segments.title: The Prohibition of Worshipping Nature: Can Mountains, Animals, and Celestial Bodies Become Forbidden Objects of Worship?

micro_segments.title: Talmudic principle: If someone worships a mountain (part of the earth), the mountain itself does not become forbidden; therefore, offerings to it are also not forbidden
Source: The verse 'their gods are on the mountains' means the gods, not the mountains themselves, are forbidden

Principle: Things that are mechubar (attached to the earth) cannot become forbidden through worship; if you worship a little worm, it can become forbidden; sun, moon, and stars are treated like mountains

Rashi's distinction: Mountains are not recognized by Halacha as objects of worship (not even objects per se), so offerings to them are not forbidden; animals are recognized as objects of worship, so offerings to them are forbidden even though the animals themselves are not
Tosafot's challenge: If worshipping a mountain is an act of avodah zarah punishable by death, how can we say the mountain is not an object of worship?
Complexity summary: Three distinct categories—personal guilt for avodah zarah, forbidding the object of worship, and forbidding the offering—do not always align consistently

macro_segments.title: Case: Slaughtering a Friend's Animal Before an Idol with Idolatrous Intent

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's ruling: When you slaughter a friend's animal (not your own) before an idol with idolatrous intent, you forbid it even though you do not own it; principle of ain adam oser davar she'eino shelo (one cannot forbid what is not theirs) is overridden by ma'aseh (action)

Ulla's principle in the name of Rabbi Yochanan: Bowing to a friend's animal does not make it forbidden (you cannot redefine someone else's property), but doing an action (ma'aseh) does make it forbidden
The logic: Simple bowing is an internal declaration you cannot impose on another's property; physical action shows control and allows you to redefine status

macro_segments.title: The Boundary Case: Rav Huna's Question—At What Point Does Slaughter Become an Offering to Avodah Zarah?

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's challenge to Rav Huna: A case of slaughtering a bird sin-offering (chatas) on Shabbat outside the Temple for idolatry—you transgress Shabbat, Temple-sacrifice rules, and avodah zarah simultaneously, all beshogeg

The paradox: If cutting one siman (windpipe/esophagus) is enough to make it an offering to avodah zarah, it is no longer a korban; so how are you chayav for shechitat kodashim bachutz (slaughtering a sacrifice outside the Temple)?

Why specifically a bird sin-offering (chatas ha'of)? A bird chatas requires only one siman; if the trachea was already half-cut and you add the smallest amount, you complete it and accomplish both the avodah zarah offering and the slaughter simultaneously

macro_segments.title: Opposing View: Can You Make a Friend's Animal Forbidden Through Action? The Debate Between Rav Huna and Rav Nachman et al.

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman, Rav Amram, and Rav Yitzchak's position: Even if you perform a full slaughter on someone else's animal, you cannot make it forbidden for avodah zarah; principle of ain adam oser davar she'eino shelo is absolute

Gemara's challenge to this view via baraita: The case of slaughtering a chatas on Shabbat outside the Temple for idolatry shows you are chayav three separate sin-offerings, implying all three transgressions take effect

Gemara's resolution: Because it is specified as a chatas (your sin-offering that atones for you), you have enough of a relationship to it to make it forbidden for avodah zarah, even though monetarily (dinei mamonot) it belongs to the kohanim

Distinction between korban and regular property: A korban, even though not yours in monetary terms, is yours in the sense of atonement and relationship, allowing you to redefine its status; a regular friend's animal remains outside your defining power

macro_segments.title: Summary of Positions: When and How Can Something Become an Offering to Avodah Zarah?

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's two innovations: (1) Even the slightest cut (beginning of slaughter) can make an animal an offering to avodah zarah; (2) You can make even a friend's animal forbidden through such action
Rav Nachman et al.'s categorical rule: You can never make a friend's animal forbidden for avodah zarah, no matter the action; ain adam oser davar she'eino shelo is absolute

Consensus on korban: Both sides agree that a korban (sacrifice) can become forbidden through even minimal action, but for different reasons—Rav Huna because it is someone else's and he allows it; the others because a korban is considered yours enough

topical_tags.term: Ain Adam Oser Davar She'eino Shelo (One Cannot Forbid What Is Not Theirs)
